div.p: Size: Exactly 64 bytes Alignment: 8-byte aligned Endianness: Little-endian Magic: "VECTORS1" = v1 format

text.tspan: // Force OS to flush all writes to physical disk
// This is the durability guarantee point
// Force OS to flush all writes to physical disk
// This is the durability guarantee point
// Either old file exists OR new file exists
// Either old file exists OR new file exists

div.p: Result: Temp file may be partial/corrupted Recovery: Original vectors.bin intact (rename never executed) Action: Delete vectors.tmp, retry
Result: Either old OR new file exists (POSIX atomic guarantee) Recovery:

ul.li: If vectors.tmp exists → delete it (incomplete rename) Action: Check both, pick complete one

div.p: Result: New vectors.bin is complete Recovery: Load new file directly Action: Normal startup

text.tspan: // Map entire file into virtual memory
// Map entire file into virtual memory

div.p: 100GB file? No problem. Only pages you touch are loaded. Mapping is instant; no read() syscall. OS prefetcher optimizes sequential access.
No double-buffering. mmap shares the OS page cache. Multiple processes share same physical pages. Contrast: read() copies kernel→userspace.
No load time. Traditional: read entire file → O(GB) mmap: just map virtual addresses → O(ms) First access triggers page fault → load.
